method2.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is an uninhabited, low-lying coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, located about 40 km south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[2] It is part of the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands, and its emergent land area encompasses 1,273 acres.[2][3] The refuge boundary includes the island and its territorial sea extending outward to the three-mile limit, covering 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters in addition to the land area.[3]

Geologically, Jarvis is the crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano.[2] The center of the island is a dried former lagoon with deep guano deposits, and past guano removal is identified as one of the major human impacts on Jarvis’s island environment.[1][2] The island has a tropical desert climate, with high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sun, scant rainfall of less than 1 m per year, and no known natural freshwater lens.[2] Its land surface is mostly sandy, rises to about 7 m at its highest point, and supports no plants larger than shrubs.[2]

The sparse terrestrial vegetation consists mainly of bunch grass, vines, and low-growing shrubs that provide nesting, roosting, and sheltering habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and numerous land hermit crabs.[2] Jarvis has more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] Management documents identify Jarvis as having 14 species of breeding seabirds and describe it as the only protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[3] The island is home to an estimated 1 million sooty terns, and eradication of invasive cats and rats has been associated with increases in many bird species and the return of shearwaters to Jarvis.[1]

The marine ecosystem around Jarvis is exceptionally productive because wind-driven equatorial upwelling combines with topographic upwelling around the island.[2] Its shallow benthic habitats include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side.[2] The fringing reef has been described as a healthy coral community, attributed to the island’s remoteness and limited anthropogenic effects.[3] Jarvis is regarded as a relatively intact terrestrial and marine tropical island ecosystem that can serve as a living laboratory for studying human impacts, coral reefs, deep marine habitats, and climate-related phenomena such as El Niño–Southern Oscillation.[3]

==History==
The first known European sighting of Jarvis Island occurred on August 21, 1821, when Captain Brown sighted it from the British ship Eliza Francis; the island was named for Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, the ship's owners.[2][4][5]

American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano deposits on the island, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed Jarvis in 1840.[2] In 1857, Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner Liholiho, took possession of Jarvis for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act, and Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS St. Mary subsequently surveyed and claimed the island for the United States.[2][4] Guano deposits were worked from 1857 until 1879, when the island was abandoned after large quantities had been removed.[4][5] One account states that about 8,000 tonnes of mined guano had been collected by the time of the abandonment.[1]

Britain annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not develop or exploit the island.[1][4][5] In 1906, it was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, but mining operations were again not developed.[4] The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in March 1935, and President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 7368 on May 13, 1936, placing control and jurisdiction of the island with the Secretary of the Interior.[1][3][4]

During World War II, Japanese forces attacked Jarvis in 1942, forcing the evacuation of the island's colonists.[1] On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its territorial sea extending to the 3-nautical-mile limit as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974 and is located about 1,305 nautical miles south of Honolulu.[2] The refuge is classified as a marine and coastal protected area in the United States Minor Outlying Islands, with a reported protected area of 1,744.87 square kilometres.[1] It includes 1,273 acres of terrestrial land and 428,580 acres of submerged lands, for a total of 429,853 acres.[2] Earlier refuge boundary language described the protected area as including all of Jarvis Island together with its territorial sea extending outward to the three-mile limit.[5] The refuge’s stated preservation purpose emphasized protection of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, with special attention to the island’s large seabird nesting colonies.[5] On January 6, 2009, the Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument was established, incorporating Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge within its boundaries.[2] The refuge is managed within the National Wildlife Refuge System mission of conserving, managing, and where appropriate restoring fish, wildlife, plants, and their habitats for the benefit of present and future generations.[2] The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has described the refuge’s core mission as protecting and preserving the natural character of its fish, wildlife, plants, coral reef communities, tidal lands, submerged lands, and waters.[2] A U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service biology team visited Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge in August 2024, the agency’s first trip there in more than six years.[3]

==Transportation==
Access to Jarvis Island is by ocean-going vessel; planning documents describe ships as the only method of visiting the island.[3] The island is about 1,263 nautical miles from refuge management staff in Honolulu, and the trip to Jarvis takes about eight days by ship on average.[3] Transportation has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels, including scientific expeditions that visit Jarvis together with other remote Pacific island refuges.[3] Refuge planning identifies the island’s remoteness, lack of affordable and reliable transportation, lack of field infrastructure, extreme environmental conditions, and landing safety concerns as major operational limitations.[3] Supplies for permitted expeditions, including fuel for power generation, must be transported and stored under strict conditions such as leak-tested U.S. Department of Transportation-approved containers, limits on total fuel stored, and secondary containment.[1]

==Military==
In 1935, the United States Department of Commerce began the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, placing U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker Islands so that weather stations and landing fields could be developed for military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[1] The project also served a strategic purpose, as the U.S. government sought to reinforce its claims to the remote islands and check Japan’s eastward territorial expansion in the Pacific.[1] The colonists, later known as Hui Panalāʻau, were primarily young Native Hawaiian men and other male students recruited from schools in Hawaii, and more than 130 young men lived on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker between 1935 and 1942.[1][2] The program expanded in 1937 to include Canton and Enderbury Islands, but ended in early 1942 during World War II.[1] Remnants of short channels blasted through Jarvis Island’s shallow fringing reefs to aid small-boat access remain visible in areas associated with historical mining and military activities.[4] Modern refuge planning notes that some hazards may still wash ashore or remain on Jarvis because of past human habitation during the guano-mining and military eras.[3]
